title: Thinking Machines Lab Unveils 'Interaction Models' — Full-Duplex AI That Listens, Interrupts, and Responds in 0.4 Seconds
why_it_matters: This is a foundational rethink of how human-AI interaction is architected — moving away from the turn-taking 'walkie-talkie' model that currently defines every major voice product. For UX and product designers building voice, multimodal, or ambient interfaces, it signals that the interaction paradigm itself is being reinvented: designing for an AI that is always present, continuously listening, and capable of interrupting mid-sentence will require entirely new patterns, flows, and conventions.
whats_happening: On May 11, 2026, Thinking Machines Lab — the AI startup founded by former OpenAI CTO Mira Murati after raising $2 billion at a $12 billion valuation — announced its first model release: a research preview of 'Interaction Models,' with its debut implementation called TML-Interaction-Small. The model is a 276-billion-parameter Mixture-of-Experts architecture (with only 12B active parameters at any time) trained from scratch to treat real-time, full-duplex interaction as a core capability rather than a bolted-on feature. Unlike every major voice AI today — which waits for a user to stop speaking before processing — Thinking Machines' model processes continuous 200-millisecond chunks of audio, video, and text simultaneously, enabling it to listen while speaking, interrupt naturally when contextually appropriate, react to visual cues, and backchannel in real time. The company claims a response latency of 0.40 seconds, comparable to the speed of natural human conversation and reportedly faster than equivalent models from OpenAI and Google. The architecture splits into a lightweight Interaction Model that stays live with the user and a Background Model that handles heavy reasoning and tool calls asynchronously, injecting results into the conversation at the right moment. The announcement is a limited research preview, not a public product — a wider release is planned for later in 2026.

title: OpenAI Codex Goes Mobile — Developers Can Now Supervise AI Coding Agents From Their Phones
why_it_matters: Codex going mobile completes a cross-device loop for AI-assisted development, turning the smartphone into a real-time command centre for autonomous coding agents. For product and engineering teams, this signals a shift toward always-on, ambient developer workflows where human oversight of AI agents is no longer tethered to a desk — a pattern that will increasingly inform how future developer tools and product dashboards are designed.
whats_happening: On May 14, 2026, OpenAI integrated its Codex AI coding agent into the ChatGPT mobile app for iOS and Android, available in preview across all plans including the free tier. Rather than enabling users to write code on their phones, the mobile integration transforms the phone into a remote control for Codex sessions already running on a Mac, Mac mini, devbox, or managed remote environment. Developers can monitor active threads, review outputs, approve commands, change models, redirect running tasks, and start new work entirely from their phone — while files, credentials, and local setup remain securely on the host machine. A QR code in the Codex Mac app links the two devices through a secure relay layer. OpenAI simultaneously announced Remote SSH general availability, Hooks now stable for all plans, and HIPAA-compliant Codex use for eligible enterprise workspaces. Codex reportedly has over 4 million weekly active users as of May 2026, with enterprise customers including Virgin Atlantic, Cisco, Notion, and Rakuten already using it in production. The move directly mirrors Anthropic's Claude Code mobile remote control feature, which launched in February 2026 — with key architectural differences: Codex runs tasks in isolated cloud sandboxes, while Claude Code runs locally with direct filesystem access.

title: Adobe Firefly Launches AI Assistant in Public Beta — A Conversational Creative Agent Across the Entire Creative Cloud
why_it_matters: Adobe's shift from generative tool to agentic creative assistant marks a significant moment for how designers interact with their primary software suite. Rather than jumping between apps to execute multi-step creative workflows, designers can now direct outcomes conversationally — a change that will accelerate production timelines but also raise new questions about creative authorship, taste, and where human judgment must remain in the loop.
whats_happening: Adobe launched the Firefly AI Assistant in public beta on April 27, 2026, positioning it as a unified conversational interface inside the Firefly app that can plan and execute complex, multi-step creative workflows across Adobe's Creative Cloud applications. Described as a 'creative agent,' the assistant allows users to describe outcomes in natural language — for example, 'remove the background and resize this for an Instagram Story' — and the AI automatically orchestrates the required steps, maintains context and progress across sessions, and surfaces decisions back to the creator. A growing library of pre-built Creative Skills enables the assistant to handle repeated tasks from a single prompt, and creators can customise their own Skills for brand-specific workflows. The launch builds on Adobe's broader 2026 Firefly push, which has expanded to over 30 partner AI models (including Google, OpenAI, Runway, Kling, and ElevenLabs), unlimited video generation on select plans, new Precision Flow image mood controls, AI Markup for targeted edits, and Adobe Brand Intelligence for on-brand content validation. Adobe has simultaneously integrated Photoshop tools into ChatGPT, signalling a strategy of 'going where creativity is' regardless of surface. The broader direction points toward AI-mediated creative pipelines where designers provide vision, judgment, and direction while the agent handles orchestration and execution.

title: HubSpot Launches Free AEO Sensor Dashboard — AI Search Visibility Becomes a Measurable Marketing Discipline
why_it_matters: As AI answer engines increasingly mediate how buyers discover products, the way brands think about digital visibility is being fundamentally restructured. For product and UX teams, this is a bellwether: the content, structure, and authority signals that make a product or brand citable in AI answers are becoming as strategically important as search rankings — and product pages, documentation, and owned content will need to be redesigned with AI citation in mind.
whats_happening: On May 14, 2026, HubSpot launched AEO Sensor, a free public dashboard that tracks real-time shifts in how AI answer engines — ChatGPT, Gemini, and Perplexity — behave toward brands across the web. The tool provides three data streams: a daily Answer Engine Volatility score (measured on a 0–100 scale from Calm to Extreme), weekly AI-referred traffic trend estimates modelled from anonymised HubSpot customer data, and industry-level visibility and citation benchmarks. The launch coincided with a striking data point from HubSpot's own infrastructure: April 2026 saw ChatGPT deliver its lowest volume of business referral traffic in the preceding 12 months. This follows a separate February 2026 study finding that ChatGPT generates approximately 190 times less traffic than Google despite accounting for around 12% of search volume. HubSpot's own customers have seen organic traffic fall 27% year-over-year, yet AEO beta users who prioritised answer engine visibility grew AI referral traffic by 20%. The Sensor sits above HubSpot's paid per-brand AEO product (launched in April) as a free macro indicator any marketer can access without a subscription, designed to help teams distinguish whether a traffic or visibility drop is brand-specific or an industry-wide AI behaviour shift.

title: Figma Slides Gets Sections — Presentation Structure Comes to the Platform's Most Collaborative Surface
why_it_matters: Sections in Figma Slides is a small but telling update that reflects a larger shift: as Figma increasingly positions itself as a complete team operating system rather than just a design tool, making presentations navigable and structurally organised is essential for product teams running strategy reviews, design crits, and stakeholder readouts entirely inside the same platform. The tighter integration between Design Mode layers and Presenter View reduces the friction of context-switching for designers who communicate through their tools as much as they design with them.
whats_happening: On May 18, 2026 — rolling out this week — Figma introduced Sections to Figma Slides, a structural update that lets teams name slide rows, drag to reorder them, and jump between sections directly from Presenter or Audience View. Sections also appear in the layers panel in Design Mode, keeping large decks navigable as they grow. The update arrives as Figma continues its push to make Slides a serious presentation layer within its broader platform ecosystem — complementing the recent Figma Make voice-to-text prompting, question cards, version history, and Zapier connector updates from earlier in May. Figma has simultaneously shipped significant performance improvements across the platform, including vector editing up to 10x faster and Make frame rates up to 4x smoother with 92% fewer memory warnings. Earlier in May, Figma also made it easier to add reference images across AI image tools in Design, Draw, Buzz, Slides, and FigJam — allowing users to click any canvas node as a reference rather than manually uploading files. Together, these updates paint a picture of Figma methodically closing UX gaps across all its surfaces as it consolidates into a unified design, prototyping, and communication platform for product teams.
